**Andronov-Hopf bifurcation** is the birth of a [[Limit Cycle]] from an equilibrium in [[Dynamical Systems Theory|dynamical systems]] generated by [[Ordinary Differential Equation|ODEs]], when the equilibrium changes stability via a pair of purely imaginary [[Linearization and the Jacobian|Eigenvalues]]. The bifurcation can be **supercritical** or **subcritical**, resulting in [[Stable Manifold|stable]] or [[Unstable Manifold|unstable]] (within an invariant two-dimensional manifold) [[Limit Cycle]], respectively.

> [![](http://www.scholarpedia.org/w/images/thumb/7/7f/SuperHopf.gif/450px-SuperHopf.gif)](http://www.scholarpedia.org/article/File:SuperHopf.gif)
> 
> *Supercritical Andronov-Hopf [bifurcation](http://www.scholarpedia.org/article/Bifurcation "Bifurcation") in the plane.*

---

## Definition

Consider an autonomous system of [[Ordinary Differential Equation]]s:

$$
\dot{x}=f(x,\alpha),\ \ \ x \in {\mathbb R}^n
$$

 depending on a parameter $Î±âˆˆR ,$ where $f$ is smooth.

- Suppose that for all sufficiently small $|\alpha|$ the system has a family of equilibria $x0(Î±) .$
- Further assume that its [[Linearization and the Jacobian|Jacobian Matrix]] $A(\alpha)=f_x(x^0(\alpha),\alpha)$ has one pair of complex [eigenvalues](http://www.scholarpedia.org/w/index.php?title=Eigenvalues&action=edit&redlink=1 "Eigenvalues (page does not exist)")

$$
\lambda_{1,2}(\alpha)=\mu(\alpha) \pm i\omega(\alpha)
$$

 that becomes purely imaginary when $Î±=0 ,$ i.e., $Î¼(0)=0$ and $Ï‰(0)=Ï‰0>0 .$ Then, generically, as $Î±$ passes through $Î±=0 ,$ the equilibrium changes [stability](http://www.scholarpedia.org/article/Stability "Stability") and a unique *limit cycle* bifurcates from it. This bifurcation is characterized by a single bifurcation condition $Re Î»1,2=0$ (has codimension one) and appears generically in one-parameter families of smooth ODEs.

## Two-dimensional Case

To describe the bifurcation analytically, consider the system above with $n=2\ ,$ 

$$
xË™1=f1(x1,x2,Î±) ,
$$

$$
xË™2=f2(x1,x2,Î±) .
$$

If the following **nondegeneracy** conditions hold:

- **(AH.1)** $l_1(0) \neq 0\ ,$ where $l1(Î±)$ is the *first Lyapunov coefficient* (see below);
- **(AH.2)** $\mu'(0) \neq 0\ ,$

then this system is locally [topologically equivalent](http://www.scholarpedia.org/article/Topological_Equivalence "Topological Equivalence") near the equilibrium to the [normal form](http://www.scholarpedia.org/article/Normal_form "Normal form") 

$$
\dot{y}_1 = \beta y_1 - y_2 + \sigma y_1(y_1^2+y_2^2)
\ ,
$$

$$
yË™2=y1+Î²y2+Ïƒy2(y21+y22) ,
$$

where $y=(y1,y2)TâˆˆR2, Î²âˆˆR ,$ and $Ïƒ=sign l1(0)=Â±1 .$

- If $\sigma=-1\ ,$ the [normal form](http://www.scholarpedia.org/article/Normal_Forms "Normal Forms") has an equilibrium at the origin, which is asymptotically stable for $Î²â‰¤0$ (weakly at $Î²=0$ ) and unstable for $Î²>0 .$ Moreover, there is a unique and stable circular limit cycle that exists for $Î²>0$ and has radius $Î²âˆš .$ This is a **supercritical** Andronov-Hopf bifurcation (see Figure [1](http://www.scholarpedia.org/article/#fig:SuperHopf.gif)).
- If $\sigma=+1\ ,$ the origin in the normal form is asymptotically stable for $Î²<0$ and unstable for $Î²â‰¥0$ (weakly at $Î²=0$ ), while a unique and unstable limit cycle exists for $Î²<0 .$ This is a **subcritical** Andronov-Hopf bifurcation (see Figure [2](http://www.scholarpedia.org/article/#fig:SubHopf.gif)).

## Multi-dimensional Case

In the $n$ -dimensional case with $nâ‰¥3 ,$ the Jacobian matrix $A0=A(0)$ has

- a simple pair of purely imaginary eigenvalues $\lambda_{1,2}=\pm i \omega_0, \ \omega_0>0\ ,$ as well as
- $n_s$ eigenvalues with $Re Î»j<0 ,$ and
- $n_u$ eigenvalues with $Re Î»j>0 ,$

with $n_s+n_u+2=n\ .$ According to the [Center Manifold Theorem](http://www.scholarpedia.org/article/Center_Manifold_Theorem "Center Manifold Theorem"), there is a family of smooth two-dimensional [invariant manifolds](http://www.scholarpedia.org/w/index.php?title=Invariant_manifolds&action=edit&redlink=1 "Invariant manifolds (page does not exist)") $WcÎ±$ near the origin. The $n$ -dimensional system restricted on $WcÎ±$ is two-dimensional, hence has the normal form above.

[![](http://www.scholarpedia.org/w/images/thumb/5/58/3DHopf.gif/500px-3DHopf.gif)](http://www.scholarpedia.org/article/File:3DHopf.gif)

Moreover, under the non-degeneracy conditions (AH.1) and (AH.2), the $n$ -dimensional system is locally [topologically equivalent](http://www.scholarpedia.org/article/Topological_Equivalence "Topological Equivalence") near the origin to the suspension of the normal form by the *standard saddle*, i.e.

$$
yË™1=Î²y1âˆ’y2+Ïƒy1(y21+y22) ,
$$

$$
yË™2=y1+Î²y2+Ïƒy2(y21+y22) ,
$$

$$
yË™s=âˆ’ys ,
$$

$$
yË™u=+yu ,
$$

where $y=(y1,y2)TâˆˆR2 ,$  $ysâˆˆRns, yuâˆˆRnu .$ Figure [3](http://www.scholarpedia.org/article/#fig:3DHopf.gif) shows the [phase portraits](http://www.scholarpedia.org/article/State_Space "State Space") of the normal form suspension when $n=3 ,$  $ns=1 ,$  $nu=0 ,$ and $Ïƒ=âˆ’1 .$

## First [[Lyapunov Exponents|Lyapunov Exponent|Lyapunov Coefficient]]

Whether Andronov-Hopf bifurcation is subcritical or supercritical is determined by $\sigma\ ,$ which is the sign of the first [[Chaos#Lyapunov Exponents|lyapunov coefficient]] $l1(0)$ of the [dynamical system](http://www.scholarpedia.org/article/Dynamical_Systems "Dynamical Systems") near the equilibrium. This coefficient can be computed at $Î±=0$ as follows. Write the [Taylor expansion](http://www.scholarpedia.org/w/index.php?title=Taylor_expansion&action=edit&redlink=1 "Taylor expansion (page does not exist)") of $f(x,0)$ at $x=0$ as 

$$
f(x,0)=A0x+12B(x,x)+16C(x,x,x)+O(âˆ¥xâˆ¥4),
$$

where $B(x,y)$ and $C(x,y,z)$ are the multilinear functions with components 

$$
Bj(x,y)=âˆ‘k,l=1nâˆ‚2fj(Î¾,0)âˆ‚Î¾kâˆ‚Î¾lâˆ£âˆ£âˆ£âˆ£Î¾=0xkyl ,
$$

$$
Cj(x,y,z)=âˆ‘k,l,m=1nâˆ‚3fj(Î¾,0)âˆ‚Î¾kâˆ‚Î¾lâˆ‚Î¾mâˆ£âˆ£âˆ£âˆ£Î¾=0xkylzm ,
$$

where $j=1,2,â€¦,n .$ Let $qâˆˆCn$ be a complex eigenvector of $A0$ corresponding to the eigenvalue $iÏ‰0 :$  $A0q=iÏ‰0q .$ Introduce also the [adjoint eigenvector](http://www.scholarpedia.org/w/index.php?title=Adjoint_eigenvector&action=edit&redlink=1 "Adjoint eigenvector (page does not exist)") $pâˆˆCn :$  $AT0p=âˆ’iÏ‰0p ,$  $âŸ¨p,qâŸ©=1 .$ Here $âŸ¨p,qâŸ©=pÂ¯Tq$ is the inner product in $Cn .$ Then (see, for example, Kuznetsov (2004)) 

$$
l1(0)=12Ï‰0Re[âŸ¨p,C(q,q,qÂ¯)âŸ©âˆ’2âŸ¨p,B(q,Aâˆ’10B(q,qÂ¯))âŸ©+âŸ¨p,B(qÂ¯,(2iÏ‰0Inâˆ’A0)âˆ’1B(q,q))âŸ©],
$$

where $In$ is the unit $nÃ—n$ matrix. Note that the value (but not the sign) of $l1(0)$ depends on the scaling of the eigenvector $q .$ The normalization $âŸ¨q,qâŸ©=1$ is one of the options to remove this ambiguity. Standard bifurcation software (e.g. [MATCONT](http://www.scholarpedia.org/article/MATCONT "MATCONT")) computes $l1(0)$ automatically.

For planar smooth ODEs with 

$$
x=\left(\begin{matrix} u \\ v \end{matrix}\right),\ \ f(x,0)=\left(\begin{matrix}
0 & -\omega_0 \\
\omega_0 & 0\end{matrix}\right)\left(\begin{matrix} u \\ v \end{matrix}\right) + 
\left(\begin{matrix} P(u,v)\\ Q(u,v)\end{matrix}\right),
$$

the setting $q=p=12âˆš(1âˆ’i)$ leads to the formula 

$$
l1(0)=18Ï‰0(Puuu+Puvv+Quuv+Qvvv)
$$

$$
+18Ï‰20[Puv(Puu+Pvv)âˆ’Quv(Quu+Qvv)âˆ’PuuQuu+PvvQvv],
$$

where the lower indices mean partial derivatives evaluated at $x=0$ (cf. Guckenheimer and Holmes, 1983).

### Some Important Examples

The first Lyapunov coefficient can be found easily in some simple but important examples (Izhikevich 2007). Here $a,b>0$ are positive parameters and all derivatives should be evaluated at the [critical equilibrium](http://www.scholarpedia.org/article/Stability_of_Equilibria "Stability of Equilibria").

| **System** | **Condition** | **${\rm sign\ }l_1(0)$** |
| --- | --- | --- |
|

$$
 \dot{x}_1 = F(x_1)-x_2 
$$

$$
 xË™2=a(x1âˆ’b) 
$$

 |

$$
 F'=0 
$$

 |

$$
 {\rm sign\ }F''' 
$$

 |

|

$$
 \dot{x}_1 = F(x_1)-x_2 
$$

$$
 xË™2=a(bx1âˆ’x2) 
$$

 |

$$
 F'=a 
$$

  and $b>a$ |

$$
 {\rm sign}\left[F'''+(F'')^2/(b-a)\right] 
$$

 |

|

$$
 \dot{x}_1 = F(x_1)-x_2 
$$

$$
 xË™2=a(G(x1)âˆ’x2) 
$$

 |

$$
 F'=a 
$$

  and $Gâ€²>a$ |

$$
 {\rm sign}\left[F'''+F''(F''-G'')/(G'-a)\right] 

$$ |

  

## Other Cases

Andronov-Hopf bifurcation occurs also in infinitely-dimensional ODEs generated by [PDEs](http://www.scholarpedia.org/article/Partial_Differential_Equations "Partial Differential Equations") and [DDEs](http://www.scholarpedia.org/article/Delay_Differential_Equations "Delay Differential Equations"), to which the [Center Manifold Theorem](http://www.scholarpedia.org/article/Center_Manifold_Theorem "Center Manifold Theorem") applies. An analogue of the Andronov-Hopf bifurcation - called **[Neimark-Sacker bifurcation](http://www.scholarpedia.org/article/Neimark-Sacker_bifurcation "Neimark-Sacker bifurcation")** - occurs in generic dynamical systems generated by iterated maps when the critical [fixed point](http://www.scholarpedia.org/article/Fixed_point "Fixed point") has a pair of simple eigenvalues $\mu_{1,2}=e^{\pm i \theta} \ .$
